tweets.text:
The US stock market has never been more dominant:

US stock market capitalization is up to a record ~$81 trillion, now accounting for ~48% of global market cap.

This exceeds the world's 2nd largest stock market, China, at $17 trillion, by 375%.

The US stock market is now twice as large as that of China, Japan, Hong Kong, and Taiwan combined.

It is also larger than the next 18 stock markets combined.

The Magnificent 7 stocks alone are now larger than China's market value.

The current scale of the US stock market is unprecedented.

tweets.text: On this day in 1785, the Continental Congress unanimously voted to adopt the dollar as the official monetary unit of the United States, replacing the chaotic mix of British pounds, Spanish reales, and state-issued paper that had circulated since independence. Thomas Jefferson proposed the decimal system, arguing that dividing a dollar into 100 cents was far simpler than the British system of pounds, shillings, and pence. The name "dollar" came from the Spanish silver dollar (itself derived from the German "thaler"), which was already the most widely circulated coin in the colonies. Congress wouldn't actually mint its own coins until the Coinage Act of 1792 established the U.S. Mint in Philadelphia. The decision to go decimal was so practical that it influenced monetary reform worldwide: France adopted a decimal currency in 1795, and most of the world eventually followed.
tweets.full_text: On this day in 1785, the Continental Congress unanimously voted to adopt the dollar as the official monetary unit of the United States, replacing the chaotic mix of British pounds, Spanish reales, and state-issued paper that had circulated since independence. Thomas Jefferson proposed the decimal system, arguing that dividing a dollar into 100 cents was far simpler than the British system of pounds, shillings, and pence. The name "dollar" came from the Spanish silver dollar (itself derived from the German "thaler"), which was already the most widely circulated coin in the colonies. Congress wouldn't actually mint its own coins until the Coinage Act of 1792 established the U.S. Mint in Philadelphia. The decision to go decimal was so practical that it influenced monetary reform worldwide: France adopted a decimal currency in 1795, and most of the world eventually followed.
